raw_results.title: The best open-source large language model
raw_results.content: # The best open-source large language model. Explore the best open-source large language models for 2025 for any budget, license, and use case. Taking a holistic view across technical specifications, customer conversations, and our own testing, we’ve put together this list of models to guide you in finding the right starting place for building on top of open source text generation models for chat, code completion, retrieval-augmented generation, and more LLM use cases. Meta's latest LLM family, Llama 3, offers 8B, 70B, and 405B parameter instruct-tuned models with excellent benchmark performance. The midsize model, Llama 3.3 70B Instruct, compares favorably to top closed-source models including GPT-4o. The models were trained on over 15 trillion tokens, with an emphasis on code and a knowledge cutoff date of December 2023 for Llama 3.3 70B (versus March 2024 for Llama 3.1 8B). What we love about Llama 3.3 70B Instruct:. What to watch out for with Llama 3.3 70B Instruct:. Llama 3.3 models have a custom commercial license that also applies to any fine-tuned derivatives.

raw_results.title: Your guide to the 6 best open-source LLMs in 2026
raw_results.content: # 6 best open-source LLMs in 2026. Learn about top open-source LLMs for 2026, their features, and how to choose the right one for your needs. Large language models (LLMs) power everything from conversational AI to data analysis. As the demand for flexible and accessible AI grows, open-source LLMs have emerged as powerful tools for businesses looking to boost efficiency, improve customer experiences, and unlock AI-driven insights. In this guide, we highlight the top six open-source LLMs of 2026 and explore how businesses can use them to scale, strengthen security, and optimize their AI infrastructure. Open-source models offer flexibility and customization, while closed-source options provide vendor support. The choice between open-source and closed-source LLMs is a defining factor in how these models can be used and adapted. In 2026, open-source LLMs are becoming the go-to choice for businesses, offering key advantages like customization, transparency, and cost-effectiveness. LLaMA 3, developed by Meta, is a powerful open-source language model optimized for text generation, sentiment analysis, and question answering across industries like healthcare, finance, and customer service.

raw_results.content: # The best open-source large language model. Explore the best open-source large language models for 2025 for any budget, license, and use case. Taking a holistic view across technical specifications, customer conversations, and our own testing, we’ve put together this list of models to guide you in finding the right starting place for building on top of open source text generation models for chat, code completion, retrieval-augmented generation, and more LLM use cases. Meta's latest LLM family, Llama 3, offers 8B, 70B, and 405B parameter instruct-tuned models with excellent benchmark performance. The midsize model, Llama 3.3 70B Instruct, compares favorably to top closed-source models including GPT-4o. The models were trained on over 15 trillion tokens, with an emphasis on code and a knowledge cutoff date of December 2023 for Llama 3.3 70B (versus March 2024 for Llama 3.1 8B). ## Summary
Recent advances in open-source language models have produced highly capable models rivaling some closed-source offerings. Notable models include Llama 3 (Meta), Mistral, DeepSeek, Qwen, GLM, and others, with varying strengths in general language tasks, coding, efficiency, and scalability. Open-source LLMs enable organizations to customize, fine-tune, and deploy high-performing NLP systems while offering cost-saving and transparency advantages.

## Key Points
- Meta's Llama 3 (including Llama 3.3 70B Instruct) excels at general NLP tasks, code generation, and offers a range of model sizes with strong benchmarks (S1, S3).
- Mistral (3B–124B parameters) provides strong performance, especially for multilingual, coding, and efficient deployment, under a permissive license (S2).
- DeepSeek-V4 and Pro are optimized for long-context reasoning, coding, and agentic workflows, with large MoE variants (S4).
- The Qwen (3.5-397B-A17B), GLM-5.2, MiniMax-M3, MiMo-V2.5, Kimi-K2.6, and Gemma 4 models are all highlighted in expert round-ups as top-performing open-source LLMs for 2026, each with different strengths and application areas (S4).
- StableLM 2 and BERT remain popular and widely integrated for NLP, translation, and code-related tasks (S2, S5).

## Important Findings
- Meta's Llama 3 models (8B, 70B, 405B) deliver state-of-the-art open-source NLP performance, rivaling top closed-source models, and are widely adopted for chat, code, and more. (S1)
- Mistral models (3B-124B) are strong in multilingual tasks, code, and efficient large-scale and edge deployments, with various licensing options. (S2)
- The top open-source LLMs of 2026 include Llama 3, which excels in text generation, sentiment analysis, and question answering across diverse industries. (S3)
- DeepSeek-V4, Qwen, GLM-5.2, MiniMax, MiMo, Kimi, and Gemma are all recent best-in-class open-source LLMs with unique strengths, such as long-context handling and coding. (S4)
- BERT and StableLM 2 APIs are still widely used for integrating open-source NLP into developer projects, supporting text generation, translation, and sentiment analysis. (S5)

## References
- S1: The best open-source large language model [general_web_search] - https://www.baseten.co/blog/the-best-open-source-large-language-model
- S2: The 11 best open-source LLMs for 2025 – n8n Blog [general_web_search] - https://blog.n8n.io/open-source-llm

## Actionable Insights
- Consider Meta's Llama 3 models for high-performance NLP and code generation, especially if instruction-tuned variants are suitable.

- DeepSeek-V4, Qwen, and MiniMax families excel in long-context, coding, and agentic workflows.
- Evaluate BERT-based models if classic, well-supported APIs or legacy compatibility is needed.

Recent advances in open-source language models have produced highly capable models rivaling some closed-source offerings. Notable models include Llama 3 (Meta), Mistral, DeepSeek, Qwen, GLM, and others, with varying strengths in general language tasks, coding, efficiency, and scalability. Open-source LLMs enable organizations to customize, fine-tune, and deploy high-performing NLP systems while offering cost-saving and transparency advantages.

## Key Points
- Meta's Llama 3 (including Llama 3.3 70B Instruct) excels at general NLP tasks, code generation, and offers a range of model sizes with strong benchmarks (S1, S3).
- Mistral (3B–124B parameters) provides strong performance, especially for multilingual, coding, and efficient deployment, under a permissive license (S2).
- DeepSeek-V4 and Pro are optimized for long-context reasoning, coding, and agentic workflows, with large MoE variants (S4).
- The Qwen (3.5-397B-A17B), GLM-5.2, MiniMax-M3, MiMo-V2.5, Kimi-K2.6, and Gemma 4 models are all highlighted in expert round-ups as top-performing open-source LLMs for 2026, each with different strengths and application areas (S4).
- StableLM 2 and BERT remain popular and widely integrated for NLP, translation, and code-related tasks (S2, S5).

## Important Findings
- Meta's Llama 3 models (8B, 70B, 405B) deliver state-of-the-art open-source NLP performance, rivaling top closed-source models, and are widely adopted for chat, code, and more. (S1)
- Mistral models (3B-124B) are strong in multilingual tasks, code, and efficient large-scale and edge deployments, with various licensing options. (S2)
- The top open-source LLMs of 2026 include Llama 3, which excels in text generation, sentiment analysis, and question answering across diverse industries. (S3)
- DeepSeek-V4, Qwen, GLM-5.2, MiniMax, MiMo, Kimi, and Gemma are all recent best-in-class open-source LLMs with unique strengths, such as long-context handling and coding. (S4)
- BERT and StableLM 2 APIs are still widely used for integrating open-source NLP into developer projects, supporting text generation, translation, and sentiment analysis. (S5)

## Summary
Recent advances in open-source language models have produced highly capable models rivaling some closed-source offerings. Notable models include Llama 3 (Meta), Mistral, DeepSeek, Qwen, GLM, and others, with varying strengths in general language tasks, coding, efficiency, and scalability. Open-source LLMs enable organizations to customize, fine-tune, and deploy high-performing NLP systems while offering cost-saving and transparency advantages.

## Key Points
- Meta's Llama 3 (including Llama 3.3 70B Instruct) excels at general NLP tasks, code generation, and offers a range of model sizes with strong benchmarks (S1, S3).
- Mistral (3B–124B parameters) provides strong performance, especially for multilingual, coding, and efficient deployment, under a permissive license (S2).
- DeepSeek-V4 and Pro are optimized for long-context reasoning, coding, and agentic workflows, with large MoE variants (S4).
- The Qwen (3.5-397B-A17B), GLM-5.2, MiniMax-M3, MiMo-V2.5, Kimi-K2.6, and Gemma 4 models are all highlighted in expert round-ups as top-performing open-source LLMs for 2026, each with different strengths and application areas (S4).
- StableLM 2 and BERT remain popular and widely integrated for NLP, translation, and code-related tasks (S2, S5).

## Important Findings
- Meta's Llama 3 models (8B, 70B, 405B) deliver state-of-the-art open-source NLP performance, rivaling top closed-source models, and are widely adopted for chat, code, and more. (S1)
- Mistral models (3B-124B) are strong in multilingual tasks, code, and efficient large-scale and edge deployments, with various licensing options. (S2)
- The top open-source LLMs of 2026 include Llama 3, which excels in text generation, sentiment analysis, and question answering across diverse industries. (S3)
- DeepSeek-V4, Qwen, GLM-5.2, MiniMax, MiMo, Kimi, and Gemma are all recent best-in-class open-source LLMs with unique strengths, such as long-context handling and coding. (S4)
- BERT and StableLM 2 APIs are still widely used for integrating open-source NLP into developer projects, supporting text generation, translation, and sentiment analysis. (S5)

## Actionable Insights
- Consider Meta's Llama 3 models for high-performance NLP and code generation, especially if instruction-tuned variants are suitable.
- Use Mistral or StableLM 2 for multilingual or efficient/edge deployment requirements.
- DeepSeek-V4, Qwen, and MiniMax families excel in long-context, coding, and agentic workflows.
- Evaluate BERT-based models if classic, well-supported APIs or legacy compatibility is needed.
